segments.paradiso-17-g25: 'Né' is a negative conjunction (nor/not even) starting the clause; 'per' is a preposition meaning 'by/through'; 'ambage' is a noun meaning 'circumlocution' or evasive speech. Together 'Né per ambage' = 'Not by circumlocution.'
segments.paradiso-17-g26: 'in che' = 'in which' introduces a relative clause; 'la gente folle' = 'the foolish people' (noun with adjective). 'già s’inviscava' uses the reflexive pronominal 's’' + imperfect 'inviscava' (to get stuck/ensnared) meaning 'were already becoming entangled.' 'pria che fosse anciso' is a temporal subordinating clause with 'pria che' + imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' + past participle 'anciso' = 'before (he) was slain' (archaic passive phrasing). 'l’Agnel di Dio che le peccata tolle' is a further relative clause: 'the Lamb of God who takes away their sins' (present, generalizing).
segments.paradiso-17-g27: 'ma' = 'but'; 'per chiare parole e con preciso latin' are adverbial/means phrases ('by clear words and in precise Latin') where 'per' and 'con' mark the manner. 'rispuose' is the literary past tense (passato remoto) of 'rispondere' = 'replied/answered.' 'quello amor paterno' is the subject phrase 'that fatherly Love' (demonstrative + noun + adjective) that does the replying.
segments.paradiso-17-g28: 'chiuso e parvente' are two participial/adjectival terms describing that love: 'chiuso' = 'closed/contained' and 'parvente' (archaic) = 'seeming/appearing.' 'del suo proprio riso' = 'of his own smile' with 'del' = di + il and the reinforced possessive 'suo proprio.' The whole phrase modifies the preceding subject (quello amor paterno).
segments.paradiso-17-g29: Opening of the direct quotation: 'La contingenza' = 'The contingency.' The article + noun phrase names the topic being announced.
segments.paradiso-17-g30: 'che' introduces a relative clause 'which'; 'fuor del quaderno' = archaic 'fuor' = 'fuori' (outside) + 'del quaderno' = 'outside the book/record.' 'de la vostra matera' = 'of your matter/subject' (de = di). 'non si stende' is present indicative with reflexive/impersonal 'si' + 'stende' = 'does not extend.' The clause says the contingency does not extend beyond that recorded matter.
segments.paradiso-17-g31: 'tutta è dipinta' = inversion for emphasis: 'all is painted/portrayed' (tutta modifies what precedes). 'nel cospetto etterno' = 'in the eternal sight/presence' (cospetto = presence/sight, etterno = archaic 'eterno'). The perfect participle 'dipinta' here conveys a timeless portrayal.
segments.paradiso-17-g32: Main clause: 'necessità' (necessity) is subject. 'però quindi' are adverbials ('therefore/from that'); 'non prende' = 'does not take/hold' (present tense). 'se non come' = 'except as' introduces a comparison: 'dal viso in che si specchia' = 'from the face in which is mirrored' (si specchia is reflexive/present meaning 'is mirrored'); 'nave che per torrente giù discende' = relative clause 'a ship which descends down a torrent' (per + noun = 'through/by means of the torrent', 'giù discende' present 'goes down'). Overall: necessity does not attach except as reflected in a face, like a ship mirrored that is swept down a torrent.
segments.paradiso-17-g33: 'Da' is the preposition 'from'; 'indi' here is the adverb 'thence/thereupon' (archaic usage meaning 'from there' or 'then'). Together 'Da indi' = 'From there' or 'thence.'
segments.paradiso-17-g34: 'sì come' = 'just as/as'; 'viene' is present 'comes/reaches'; 'ad orecchia' = 'to the ear' (ad + singular noun used idiomatically); 'dolce armonia da organo' = 'sweet harmony from an organ' (da = from). The phrase is a simile comparing the way the time appears to the way music reaches the ear.
segments.paradiso-17-g35: 'mi viene a vista' is an impersonal/experiential phrasing: 'it comes to my sight' or 'appears to me' ('mi' = to me, 'viene' = comes, 'a vista' = to view). 'il tempo' is the grammatical subject that 'comes'; 'che ti s’apparecchia' is a relative clause: 'that prepares itself for you' ('ti' = for you, 's’' reflexive, 'apparecchia' = readies/prepares).
segments.paradiso-17-g36: 'Qual' here means 'just as'/'as was the case when'; 'si partio' is the literary past (passato remoto) reflexive-ish form meaning 'departed/left.' 'Ipolito d’Atene' names the example (Hippolytus of Athens). 'per la spietata e perfida noverca' gives the reason 'because of the merciless and treacherous stepmother' (per = because of/in consequence of).
segments.paradiso-17-g37: 'tal' = 'such/so'; 'di Fiorenza' = 'from Florence' (di marking origin); 'partir' is the infinitive 'to leave' used after the impersonal/fitting verb; 'ti convene' is present of 'convenire' meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate for you' (literary word order with infinitive before the impersonal verb). So: 'so, you must leave Florence.'
segments.paradiso-17-g38: 'Questo si vuole' is impersonal/passive: 'this is wanted/desired' (si + verb). 'e questo già si cerca' repeats the structure: 'and this is already sought' ('già' = already). The 'si' makes both verbs impersonal or passive in meaning.
segments.paradiso-17-g39: 'e tosto verrà fatto' = 'and soon it will be done' (future passive periphrasis: 'verrà' + past participle 'fatto'). 'a chi ciò pensa' = 'to whoever thinks this' ('a' marks the beneficiary/recipient). 'là dove Cristo tutto dì si merca' = 'there where Christ all day plies his trade' — 'tutto dì' = 'all day', 'si merca' (literary/medieval) = 'does his trading/markets' (here figurative).
segments.paradiso-17-g40: Main clause: the future verb seguirà (third-person singular future) takes la colpa as subject and la parte offensa as the following noun phrase; ti/indirect objects are absent so seguirà = 'will follow'. The prepositional phrase in grido attaches to the verb phrase to indicate manner ('with an outcry'). Word order is subject–verb–object with the manner phrase after the object.
segments.paradiso-17-g41: Short adverbial comparative: come suol = 'as is wont' or 'as usually happens'. Suol is an impersonal/habitual form (from solevare/solere) indicating customary action; come introduces the simile.
segments.paradiso-17-g42: Contrast clause introduced by ma ('but'). Fia is an archaic future-subjunctive/indicative form of essere used here as future ('will be'); testimonio is predicate nominative. The phrase al ver che la dispensa is a prepositional phrase ('to the truth that dispenses it') where che introduces a relative clause modifying ver.
segments.paradiso-17-g43: Second-person future lascerai governs the direct object ogne cosa diletta ('every beloved thing'). Più caramente is an adverbial phrase modifying lascerai and conveys 'at greater cost' or 'more painfully.' Tu is the explicit subject; word order subject–verb–object is regular.
segments.paradiso-17-g44: Copular clause: 'e questo è quello strale' (this is that arrow) with a relative clause introduced by che. In the relative clause l’arco de lo essilio is the subject ('the bow of exile') and pria saetta is the verb phrase: pria (an adverb 'first/before') + saetta (here a verb meaning 'shoots'); the relative clause thus specifies which 'arrow' is meant.
segments.paradiso-17-g45: Proverai is 2nd-person singular future meaning 'you will experience/learn.' The sequence sì come + clause functions as a comparative/explanatory introducer ('how/just as'), and sa here is a present third-person with the sense 'tastes (of)'. Di sale ('of salt') is the complement to sa; lo pane altrui is the noun phrase 'another's bread.'
segments.paradiso-17-g46: Parallel comparative: e come è duro calle introduces an exclamatory/comparative clause ('and how hard a road it is'). The abstract nominal phrase lo scendere e ’l salir uses infinitives with the definite article (lo, ’l = il) to nominalize them ('the descending and the ascending'). Per l’altrui scale indicates the route ('by/through another's stairs'); word order places the nominalized infinitives before the prepositional phrase.
segments.paradiso-17-g47: Relative-nominal construction: 'Quel che' = 'that which/what' is the head of the clause; graverà is future third-person singular and takes ti as the indirect object ('will weigh on you') and le spalle as the thing weighed upon ('your shoulders'). The comparative più modifies graverà to indicate 'most/heaviest.'
segments.paradiso-17-g48: Copular verb sarà links the predicate la compagnia malvagia e scempia to the subject quel che...; the phrase con la qual introduces a relative clause ('with which') whose verb is cadrai (future 2nd person), so the company is the ensemble 'with which you will fall into this valley.' The adjective scempia is pejorative; malvagia is an adjectival form 'wicked.'
segments.paradiso-17-g49: Continuation of the relative clause: che introduces a further descriptive clause; tutta is an intensive adverb/adjective meaning 'entirely/wholly' and ingrata is an adjective ('ungrateful/thankless') modifying the company.
segments.paradiso-17-g50: Coordinated adjectives: tutta matta ed empia modifies the company; si farà is a reflexive/periphrastic future ('will make itself / will become') meaning 'will set itself' or 'will show itself' against te. Contr' a is an archaic contraction of contro a ('against'), so the whole clause means they will turn against you.
segments.paradiso-17-g51: Simple adversative conjunction 'ma' introducing the following contrast (but...).
segments.paradiso-17-g52: Adverbial phrase 'poco appresso' = 'shortly after' or 'soon afterwards', setting a temporal relation to the previous action.
segments.paradiso-17-g53: Pronoun ella ('she' or 'it') resumes reference to the previously personified company; position at the start of the clause gives it topical/emphatic force.
segments.paradiso-17-g54: Short contrastive phrase 'non tu' ('not you') — inversion for emphasis: the following clause will state that the company, and not you, will undergo the described change.
segments.paradiso-17-g55: Compact clause with the clitic n’ (ne) + avrà: n’ avrà = ne avrà ('will have of it' or 'will have because of it'); rossa la tempia places the adjective rossa after the verb with the noun phrase la tempia as object/complement ('will have the temple red' = 'will have her temple reddened'). The construction indicates the consequence will fall on her rather than on you.
segments.paradiso-17-g118: Ché is a causal conjunction ('for'/'because') introducing the reason. se is the conditional 'if' introducing the protasis. La voce tua is a normal noun phrase with the definite article before the noun and the possessive after it (tua after voce). Sarà is the future indicative of essere ('will be') agreeing with voce; molesta is an adjective complement meaning 'troublesome'. Nel primo gusto is a temporal/experiential phrase ('in the first taste/at first hearing'), with the contraction nel = in + il.
segments.paradiso-17-g119: The noun phrase 'vital nodrimento' functions as the object: vital (an attributive adjective) modifies nodrimento 'nourishment'. Lascerà is future indicative of lasciare ('will leave/leave behind'); poi is an adverb 'afterward/then' that places the action in time.
segments.paradiso-17-g120: Quando introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. Sarà is again future indicative of essere; digesta is a past participle used adjectivally ('digested'), agreeing with the implied object/nodrimento, so the clause means 'when it has been digested.'
segments.paradiso-17-g121: Questo tuo grido is a demonstrative + possessive + noun ('this cry of yours'). Farà is future of fare and here means 'will act/serve' (literally 'will make/does') followed by come + noun to make a simile: 'will act like the wind.' Come introduces the comparison and vento is the noun compared to the cry.
segments.paradiso-17-g122: Che introduces a relative clause modifying vento ('which'). Le più alte cime is a feminine plural NP with two instances of più intensifying 'alte' and 'percuote' is present indicative, third person singular, meaning 'strikes' or 'assails'; the second più here means 'to a greater degree' ('the higher...the more it strikes'). Word order places the comparative focus on the adjective and adverb.
segments.paradiso-17-g123: E begins the clause 'and'. Ciò is a demonstrative pronoun 'that/this' referring to the preceding idea; non fa is a negative with fare meaning 'does not make' or 'is not.' D’onor (di onor) is a genitive/partitive 'of honor' and poco argomento is a phrase 'little argument' meaning 'no small proof' when taken with negation — idiomatically 'not a small argument (i.e., not insignificant) of honor.'
segments.paradiso-17-g124: Però is a discourse adverb meaning 'therefore' or 'however' depending on context; here 'therefore.' Ti is the indirect object pronoun 'to you' placed before the verb (typical in older/poetic Italian). Son (sono) is the present plural of essere used impersonally with the past participle mostrate: 'have been shown' (literal order: 'to you are shown'). Mostrate agrees in gender/number with the omitted plural subject (rote). In queste rote is a locative prepositional phrase specifying where.
segments.paradiso-17-g125: Nel monte e ne la valle dolorosa lists two locations ('on the mountain and in the dolorous valley'); note the older prepositional form ne la = in + the. Pur here is an adverb meaning 'even'/'still' emphasizing the following noun. L'anime is the plural of anima with the elided article l'; che son di fama note is a relative clause 'who are noted for fame' (son = are, di fama = 'of fame', note = 'noted/famous').
segments.paradiso-17-g126: Che introduces a new subordinate clause explaining why the famous souls are shown. L'animo di quel ch'ode is 'the mind/spirit of the one who hears': l' animo = the spirit/mind, di quel = 'of him who', ch'ode = contraction of che + ode 'who hears.' The whole phrase is the subject of the following verbs.
segments.paradiso-17-g127: Non posa né ferma fede is a coordinate negative: non posa 'does not rest' (posa = present indicative 'rests/settles'), né ... ferma fede 'nor does it establish firm faith' — ferma here is adjectival in the phrase 'ferma fede' ('firm faith'), and fede is the noun. Per essempro introduces an exemplifying causal phrase 'for example' or 'by example'; ch'aia is a contracted form of che abbia (subjunctive) meaning 'that has' or 'though it may have' and introduces the subordinate describing the root (la sua radice incognita e ascosa). The whole construction explains that the hearer's spirit does not settle its faith if, for example, the root is unknown and hidden.
segments.paradiso-17-g128: Né per altro argomento 'nor by any other argument' continues the negative coordination: né ... per altro argomento. Che non paia is a relative clause with che introducing the clause and paia representing the present subjunctive of parere ('seem'); the negation non reverses the meaning: 'that does not seem' or 'that is not apparent.' The overall sense: nor on any other argument that does not appear convincing.
segments.paradiso-17-g71: Le is the feminine plural definite article agreeing with magnificenze; sue is the possessive adjective agreeing in gender/number with magnificenze (feminine plural). Conosciute is a past participle used post‑nominally to modify magnificenze (agreement in gender/number), and saranno is the 3rd person plural future of essere ('will be'); ancora follows the verb here with the sense 'still' (it modifies the whole predicate: 'will still be known').
segments.paradiso-17-g72: Sì che is a result/consequence connector ('so that'); the following che is the complementizer 'that'. '’ suoi' shows an elision/apostrophe used in poetry (originally ai/i suoi etc. depending on metre) but functions as the possessive suo ('his') modifying nemici. Non is the sentential negation placed before the clitic ne and the verb; ne is the clitic pronoun referring back to the magnificenze ('of/about them'); potran is the archaic/poetic 3rd person plural future of potere ('they will be able'); tener is the infinitive governed by potran ('to keep'); le lingue mute is the direct object with the adjective mute post‑nominally describing lingue ('tongues' = 'speech'), so literally 'keep the tongues silent (about them)'.
segments.paradiso-17-g73: A lui is a prepositional phrase 'to/for him' (a + lui). t’ is the clitic form of ti (you) attached to the verb, so t’aspetta = ti aspetta 'awaits you' (3rd person singular verb aspettare); e = 'and'. a’ suoi benefici shows the poetic/aphetic apostrophe: a' = ai (to the) or a + elided vowel used for metre; suoi benefici = 'his benefits/benefices' (possessive suo agreeing with benefici). The clause therefore communicates who and what await or concern that person.
segments.paradiso-17-g74: Per lui is an agent/means phrase 'by/through him'. Fia here is a future form of essere used in Dante's diction ('will be' / 'shall be'); trasmutata is a past participle agreeing with gente (feminine singular collective) and means 'transformed/changed'; molta modifies gente and means 'many' (or 'much' but here 'many people'); gente = 'people'.
segments.paradiso-17-g75: Cambiando is a gerund ('changing') that gives the circumstance or result of the previous clause. Condizion (an older orthography of condizione) means 'condition' or more specifically 'social status' here; ricchi e mendici are the two groups being contrasted (ricchi = 'the rich', mendici = 'beggars'), with the adjective/noun order typical of Dante's diction.
segments.paradiso-17-g76: E = 'and'. Portera’ is a future 3rd person singular of portare ('will carry' / 'will bear'); the apostrophe marks a poetic elision (porterà). Ne is the clitic pronoun 'of/about it/them' referring back to what was changed; scritto here is a past participle used substantively or adverbially meaning 'written'/'recorded'; the following ne (here an archaic prepositional variant) + la mente di lui = 'in/within his mind' (ne la mente = in the mind), so the phrase says they will bear it recorded in his mind (i.e., it will be kept in memory of him).
segments.paradiso-17-g77: E = 'and'. Nol is the fused negative clitic (non + lo) meaning 'not it' placed before the verb in older/poetic syntax; dirai is 2nd person singular future of dire ('you will say'), so nol dirai = 'you will not say it'. The order (clitic before verb) is a feature of older Italian/poetic style.
segments.paradiso-17-g78: E = 'and'. Disse is the passato remoto (3rd sg.) of dire ('he said'). Cose = 'things' and incredibili = 'incredible/unbelievable', both standard uses. A introduces the indirect object 'to those (quei) who were present'; che is the relative 'who/which/that'; presente is 'present' (here 'present [at the event]'). The combination says that he spoke unbelievable things to those present.
segments.paradiso-17-g79: Poi is a temporal adverb meaning 'then' (introducing what follows). Giunse is the passato remoto (third singular) of giungere, narrative past: 'he/it came' — subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.paradiso-17-g80: Figlio is a vocative noun (address) meaning 'son' or 'child'; it stands alone as direct address to the addressee.
segments.paradiso-17-g81: Queste (feminine plural) agrees with chiose and means 'these.' Son is the contracted poetic form of sono ('are'). Le is the feminine plural article pointing to the following noun. Chiose here is an archaic/poetic plural (cf. 'chiosa') meaning 'glosses' or 'explanations' (the provided sense list does not include this meaning). Di quel che introduces a relative content: 'of that which'; ti fu detto is a passive/perfect construction meaning 'was told to you' — fu is the passato remoto auxiliary 'was' and detto is the past participle of dire.
segments.paradiso-17-g82: Ecco is an interjection meaning 'here is/are.' Le is the article for insidie. ’Nsidie (poetic elision of 'insidie') means 'snares/ambushes.' Che introduces the relative clause. Dietro a pochi giri is an adverbial phrase: dietro a + plural noun here means 'after/behind a few turns' (a few turns = giri, metaphorical steps or stages). Son nascose = 'are hidden' where son (sono) is present 'are' and nascose is the past participle/adjectival form agreeing with feminine plural insidie.
segments.paradiso-17-g83: Non negates the verb. Vo' is an archaic contracted form of voglio (I want); here it means 'I wish' or 'I want' and is negated by Non — 'I do not want.' Però is 'however' or 'nevertheless' qualifying the statement. Ch' is the clitic form of che introducing a subordinate clause. A marks the indirect object 'to/for' (here: directed toward). Tuoi vicini is 'your neighbors' (tuoi = possessive, vicini = neighbors). Invidie is a plural noun 'envies' or 'ill will' — 'that envy (should be directed) at your neighbors.'
segments.paradiso-17-g84: Poscia che is a conjunction meaning 'since' or 'after that.' S’infutura is reflexive/clitic s’ + the verb infuturare (poetic/archaic) meaning 'become future' or 'happen in the future' — here 'will henceforth be/occur'; the construction expresses a future/inceptive sense for 'la tua vita.' La tua vita is 'your life.' Vie più là is an adverbial phrase 'ever/farther on' (vie is 'via' used adverbially to mean 'further/along'). Che in this clause is comparative 'than.' ’L is the elided article il (the). Punir is a substantivized infinitive meaning 'punishment' or 'avenging' in older usage. Di lor perfidie = 'of their perfidies/treacheries.' The whole clause says your life will henceforth extend farther than the punishment of their treacheries.
segments.paradiso-17-g1: 'Qual' here functions like 'as one who' (a comparative/demo pronoun). 'Venne' is the literary past tense (past historic) meaning 'came' or 'went'; 'a Climenè' is the prepositional phrase 'to Climenè' marking destination.
segments.paradiso-17-g2: 'Per accertarsi' = 'in order to ascertain' (per + reflexive infinitive of purpose). The subordinate clause 'di ciò ch’avea ... udito' uses the imperfect 'avea udito' = 'had heard', and 'incontro a sé' means 'concerning himself' or 'about himself'.
segments.paradiso-17-g3: 'Quei' is a demonstrative/pronominal 'the sort of person' or 'he who'; the relative clause 'ch’ancor fa li padri ai figli scarsi' describes a habitual action: 'what fathers still do for their needy/scanty children'. 'Fa' is present/generic 'do', and 'scarsi' qualifies 'figli' as 'lacking/poor'.
segments.paradiso-17-g4: Simple copular statement: 'tal era io' = 'such was I' (imperfect 'era' describing past state). 'Tal' (= 'such') resumes the preceding comparison.
segments.paradiso-17-g5: 'E tal era sentito' = 'and such was (how) I was perceived/known'; 'era sentito' is a passive/perceptual construction. The agents follow with 'da Beatrice e da la santa lampa' = 'by Beatrice and by the holy lamp'. The relative clause 'che pria per me avea mutato sito' uses the imperfect 'avea mutato' ('had changed') — 'pria' = 'earlier' and 'per me' = 'for my sake'.
segments.paradiso-17-g6: 'Per che' here functions as 'therefore/for that reason'. The command in direct speech 'Manda fuor la vampa del tuo disio' uses the imperative 'manda' = 'send forth' and the metaphorical 'vampa del tuo disio' = 'the blaze of your desire'.
segments.paradiso-17-g7: 'Mi disse' = 'she said to me'; 'mi' = indirect object 'to me' and 'disse' is the past historic 'said', introducing the reported speech.
segments.paradiso-17-g8: 'Sì ch’ella esca' is a result/purpose clause: 'so that it may come forth' — 'esca' is a present subjunctive (jussive/result). 'Segnata bene de la interna stampa' = 'well marked by the internal stamp/imprint' — 'segnata' is a past participle describing the flame as stamped.
segments.paradiso-17-g9: 'Non perché nostra conoscenza cresca per tuo parlare' is a negative purpose clause: 'not that our knowledge should grow by your speaking'. 'Perché' introduces the clause; 'cresca' is present subjunctive expressing potential/result, and 'per tuo parlare' indicates instrument/cause ('through your speaking').
segments.paradiso-17-g10: 'Ma perché t’ausi a dir la sete' = 'but so that you may dare to speak of your thirst'. 'Ausi' (from 'audere') is a present-subjunctive-form notion here with jussive/result sense 'may dare'; 'a dir' is the infinitive 'to say'.
segments.paradiso-17-g11: 'Sì che l’uom ti mesca' is a result clause: 'so that a man may mingle you / may mix you' (the exact nuance of 'mesca' is ambiguous here; it expresses a resultant action by 'l'uom'). 'Sì che' introduces the consequence; 'mesca' is a present-subjunctive/resultive verb form.
segments.paradiso-17-g12: Address/affectionate apostrophe: 'O cara piota mia' = 'O my dear pious one of mine'; 'sì t'insusi' = 'so unaccustomed to yourself' — 'insusi' (from 'insuso') is an adjective meaning 'unused/unaccustomed to'. 'Sì' is an intensifier 'so'.
segments.paradiso-17-g13: Simple connective 'che' introducing the explanatory/simile clause that follows ('for, that' / 'in that').
segments.paradiso-17-g14: 'Come ... non capere in trïangol due ottusi' is a simile: 'as (it is) seen by earthly minds that two obtuse angles cannot be contained in a triangle.' 'Come' = 'as'; 'non capere' = 'cannot be contained/understood' (here 'capere' = 'to contain/understand'); 'ottusi' refers to obtuse angles.
segments.paradiso-17-g15: 'Così vedi le cose contingenti anzi che sieno in sé' continues the simile: 'so you see contingent things before they are in themselves.' 'Anzi che' is temporal = 'before'; 'sieno' is present subjunctive 'they be' in the subordinate clause.
segments.paradiso-17-g16: 'Mirando il punto a cui tutti li tempi son presenti' = 'by gazing at the point to which all times are present.' 'Mirando' is a gerund meaning 'gazing'; 'a cui' is the relative pronoun 'to which'; 'tutti li tempi son presenti' states that at that point all times are present.
segments.paradiso-17-g17: 'Mentre ch’io era a Virgilio congiunto su per lo monte che l’anime cura e discendendo nel mondo defunto' sets the scene: 'while I was joined to Virgil up the mountain that cares for souls, and descending into the dead world.' 'Era' imperfect indicates the ongoing past state; 'congiunto' is past participle used adjectivally; 'su per lo monte' = 'up the mountain'; 'discendendo' is a gerund marking simultaneous descent.
segments.paradiso-17-g18: 'Dette mi fuor di mia vita futura parole gravi' = 'words concerning my future life were said to me — grave words.' 'Dette' (feminine pl.) and 'fuor' (short for 'furon') combine to mean 'were said'; 'di mia vita futura' specifies their topic; 'parole gravi' = 'serious/weighty words.'
segments.paradiso-17-g19: 'Avvegna ch’io mi senta ben tetragono ai colpi di ventura' is concessive: 'although I feel myself well braced/firm against the blows of fortune.' 'Avvegna ch’' introduces an 'although' clause; 'mi senta' = 'I feel' (with a subjunctive flavor in Dante), and 'tetragono' = 'made firm/rigid' against adversity.
segments.paradiso-17-g20: 'Per che la voglia mia saria contenta d’intender qual fortuna mi s’appressa' = 'therefore my will would be satisfied to understand what fate approaches me.' 'Saria' is the conditional 'would be', 'd'intender' = 'to understand', and 'mi s'appressa' = 'approaches me' (reflexive/applicative form).
segments.paradiso-17-g21: 'Ché saetta previsa vien più lenta' is a proverb-like closing: 'for a foreseen shaft/bolt comes more slowly.' 'Previsa' = 'foreseen' and 'vien' = 'comes'; the idea is that something anticipated seems to come more slowly.
segments.paradiso-17-g22: 'Così diss' io a quella luce stessa' = 'Thus I said to that very light.' 'Diss'' is the archaic contracted past 'dissi' (I said). 'Che pria m'avea parlato' is a relative clause: 'which had earlier spoken to me' — 'pria' = 'earlier' and 'm'avea parlato' = pluperfect 'had spoken to me'.
segments.paradiso-17-g23: 'E come volle Beatrice' = 'and as Beatrice wished' — 'volle' is the past historic 'willed/desired', indicating that what follows was done according to Beatrice's wish.
segments.paradiso-17-g24: 'Fu la mia voglia confessa' = 'my desire was confessed/made known.' 'Fu' is past historic of 'essere' ('was'), and 'confessa' means 'acknowledged' or 'declared'. The passive-like phrasing reports that his desire was made public/clear.
segments.paradiso-17-g56: Di sua bestialitate is a prepositional phrase (“of/with respect to his beastliness”) that marks the matter the following clause treats. Il suo processo is the subject (the trial/process concerning him); farà la prova is a future indicative (farà = from fare) used idiomatically with la prova to mean “will provide proof / will put to the test.” Word order is inverted for poetic effect (topic phrase first).
segments.paradiso-17-g57: sì ch’a te fia bello is a result clause: sì ch’ = “so that,” a te = dative “to you,” and fia is an archaic/fixed form of ‘will be’ (subjunctive/future sense). Bello is a predicative adjective meaning “pleasant/agreeable.” Averti fatta parte is an infinitive construction with avere + enclitic pronoun (averti = avere + te) plus the past participle fatta agreeing with parte (feminine): literally “to have made a part for you” = “to have gained/secured a share for yourself.” Per te stesso specifies “by/for yourself.”
segments.paradiso-17-g58: Lo primo tuo refugio e ’l primo ostello is a doubled subject (“your first refuge and your first lodging”). Sarà is future of essere linking to the predicate la cortesia del gran Lombardo (“the favor/courtesy of the great Lombard”). The relative clause che ’n su la scala porta il santo uccello modifies Lombardo; ’n su is a contracted, archaic in su meaning “on/upon.”
segments.paradiso-17-g59: ch’in te avrà sì benigno riguardo is a relative clause: che = “who/which,” in te = “toward you,” avrà is future of avere (“will have”), and sì benigno riguardo = “so kindly a regard” (sì intensifies benigno). The clause says the Lombard will look on you with kindly regard.
segments.paradiso-17-g60: che del fare e del chieder is a continuation specifying the sphere of action: che = “in regard to/so that as for,” del fare = “of the doing” and del chieder = “of the asking/requesting.” Fare and chieder are infinitives used substantively (nominalized) after del.
segments.paradiso-17-g61: tra voi due is a simple prepositional phrase “between you two” (tra = between, voi = you, due = two). It identifies the two people whose doings/requests are being compared.
segments.paradiso-17-g62: fia primo quel che tra li altri è più tardo is a result/provisory ordering: fia (future of essere used in this sort of indirect clause) = “will be,” primo = “first,” quel = demonstrative “that one,” and the relative clause che tra li altri è più tardo = “which among the others is the more slow/late.” In sense: of the two, the one who is slower among others will be served first.
segments.paradiso-17-g63: Con lui vedrai colui che ’mpresso fue is: Con lui = “with him,” vedrai = future of vedere (“you will see”), colui che = “the one who,” and ’mpresso fue is an archaic passive/perfect (“was impressed/marked”)—fue is an older past-tense form of essere. The clause introduces a person characterized as being impressed at birth.
segments.paradiso-17-g64: nascendo is a gerund (nascere → nascendo = “being born / at the time of his birth”) used adverbially to indicate when the impressing occurred.
segments.paradiso-17-g65: sì da questa stella forte = “so (much) by/from this strong star.” Sì intensifies the following causal/prepositional phrase; da indicates source/influence (the star’s influence), questa stella forte = “this powerful/star of force.”
segments.paradiso-17-g66: che notabili fier l’opere sue is a result clause: che = “that,” notabili (plural) agrees with opere = “notable/remarkable,” and fier is an archaic form from Latin fio (“to become/made”), here meaning “became” or “were made.” L’opere sue = “his works.”
segments.paradiso-17-g67: Non se ne son le genti ancora accorte per la novella età: non ... son ... accorte is the negated copular construction “are not yet aware”; se ne = pronominal combination referring back to the matter (“of it/about it”); le genti = “the people”; ancora = “still/yet”; accorte is past participle used adjectivally = “aware”; per la novella età = “because of the new/young age” (novella età = youth).
segments.paradiso-17-g68: ché pur nove anni son queste rote intorno di lui torte: ché = “for/because”; pur = “only/even/just” here emphasizing brevity; nove anni son = inversion for “are nine years” (i.e. nine years have passed); queste rote intorno di lui torte = poetic expression “these spheres/wheels have been turned about him” (torte = turned/twisted, participial adjective).
segments.paradiso-17-g69: ma pria che ’l Guasco l’alto Arrigo inganni: ma = “but”; pria che = “before” followed by a subjunctive/verb of potentiality; ’l Guasco = nickname/title (the Gascon/braggart); l’alto Arrigo = “the lofty Henry”; inganni is the verb (subjunctive/optative sense) meaning “deceives” or “should deceive” in the subordinate temporal clause.
segments.paradiso-17-g70: parran faville de la sua virtute in non curar d’argento né d’affanni: parran (poetic future) = “sparks will appear/they will seem” (subject faville); faville = “sparks” (metaphorical for hints); de la sua virtute = “of his virtue”; in non curar d’argento né d’affanni = “in not caring for silver nor for worries/troubles” (an infinitival noun phrase describing the manner in which the sparks will appear).
segments.paradiso-17-g85: "Poi che," is a temporal/concessive conjunction meaning 'after' or 'since' used to introduce what follows; word order places the conjunction at the start of the clause for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-17-g86: "tacendo" is the gerund of tacere ('to be silent'), used adverbially to mean 'being silent' or 'while remaining silent'; it modifies the main verb that follows.
segments.paradiso-17-g87: "si mostrò spedita" is a reflexive/passive-like construction: si + mostrar(si) in the passato remoto (mostrò) meaning 'showed itself/appeared'; "spedita" here is an adjective meaning 'ready/quick' (archaic/poetic), and the following noun phrase names who is ready and for what action.
segments.paradiso-17-g88: "io cominciai" is a simple past (passato remoto) verb phrase: subject pronoun io + cominciai ('I began'), used to start Dante's reported speech/action.
segments.paradiso-17-g89: "come colui che brama" is a simile: come + colui 'he who/one who' + relative che + brama (present indicative) meaning 'like one who longs'; word order is standard for a simile introducing Dante's state of mind.
segments.paradiso-17-g90: "dubitando" is a gerund (dubitare) used adverbially: 'doubting' or 'hesitating', modifying the main verb of the clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-17-g91: "consiglio da persona che vede e vuol dirittamente e ama" is a noun phrase functioning as the object of cominciai: consiglio 'advice' introduced with da (from/by) + persona 'a person' qualified by a relative clause (che vede e vuol dirittamente e ama) where vede and vuol and ama are present-tense verbs describing that person.
segments.paradiso-17-g92: "Ben veggio" is a fixed colloquial opening: Ben (emphatic 'well/indeed') + veggio (present indicative 'I see' or 'I perceive'), used to introduce what he is about to say.
segments.paradiso-17-g93: "padre mio" is a direct vocative address ('my father'), with possessive mio after the noun as is normal in Italian.
segments.paradiso-17-g94: "sì come sprona lo tempo verso me" is a comparative/explanatory clause: sì come ('just as') + sprona (present indicative 'spurs/urges on') + lo tempo verso me ('time toward me'), giving the motive or observation.
segments.paradiso-17-g95: "per colpo darmi tal" uses per + noun (colpo 'a blow') + infinitive construction darmi ('to give me') and tal ('such') — a compressed poetic word order meaning 'to give me such a blow'.
segments.paradiso-17-g96: "ch’è più grave a chi più s’abbandona" is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by ch’ (che) + essere (è) + comparative più + adjective grave ('grave/serious/burdensome'), with a dative-like clause a chi ('to whoever') followed by a reflexive pronominal verb s'abbandona ('abandons himself'); the structure explains that the blow is heavier for those who surrender themselves more.
segments.paradiso-17-g97: "per che di provedenza è buon ch’io m’armi" is a purposive/explanatory clause: per che ('therefore/for which reason') + di provedenza ('by/of providence' or 'out of foresight') + è buon ch’io m’armi (impersonal 'it is good that I arm myself'); ch’io m’armi uses the subordinate conjunction che + present subjunctive armi (armare) in the sense 'that I provide/prepare myself'.
segments.paradiso-17-g98: "sì che," is a connective 'so that' or 'therefore' introducing the consequent clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-17-g99: "se loco m’è tolto più caro," is a conditional clause: se ('if') + subject 'loco' 'place' + clitic phrase m’è tolto ('is taken from me' — passive/perfect) + più caro ('more dear'); word order is compact and poetic, with the indirect object clitic attached to the verb.
segments.paradiso-17-g100: "io non perdessi li altri per miei carmi." is a purpose/negative fear formula using the imperfect subjunctive perdessi ('I might lose') after a desiderative/optative sense; the phrase means 'that I might not lose the others because of my verses' — carmi is the poetical plural for 'poems/verses'.
segments.paradiso-17-g101: "Giù per lo mondo sanza fine amaro" is a prepositional and adverbial phrase: Giù ('down') + per lo mondo ('through the world') + sanza (archaic for senza, 'without') + fine amaro ('bitter end'); the adjective amaro qualifies fine and the expression is idiomatic/poetic.
segments.paradiso-17-g102: "e per lo monte del cui bel cacume li occhi de la mia donna mi levaro" is a coordinated prepositional phrase: e per lo monte ('and across the mountain') + del cui bel cacume ('whose beautiful summit') + relative clause li occhi ... mi levaro (levaro is passato remoto 'lifted me' or 'raised me'); the construction uses poetic inversion and an impersonal plural article li for 'the.'
segments.paradiso-17-g103: "e poscia per lo ciel," is a temporal sequence connector: e poscia ('and afterwards') + per lo ciel ('through the heavens'), continuing the itinerary; poscia is an archaic/poetic form for 'afterwards'.
segments.paradiso-17-g104: "di lume in lume" is an ablative-like phrase meaning 'from light to light' or 'from one heaven-light to another,' using repetition di + noun + in + noun to indicate successive stages.
segments.paradiso-17-g105: "ho io appreso quel che s’io ridico" uses ho appreso (present perfect 'I have learned') + quod-clause quel che ('what that') + s'io ridico (conditional 'if I retell' where s' stands for se 'if' and ridico is present; the sequence marks learned knowledge and the hypothetical effect of recounting it).
segments.paradiso-17-g106: "a molti fia sapor di forte agrume;" is a predictive clause: a molti ('to many') + fia (future of essere 'will be') + sapor ('a taste') di forte agrume ('of strong sourness/citrus'); agrume here is used metaphorically for a sharp/sour flavor.
segments.paradiso-17-g107: "e s’io al vero son timido amico," is a concessive/conditional preface: e ('and') + s'io (se io 'if I') + al vero ('in truth' or 'as to the truth') + son (son = sono 'I am') + timido amico ('a timid friend'); the contracted s'io is a common poetic abbreviation for se io.
segments.paradiso-17-g108: "temo di perder viver tra coloro che questo tempo chiameranno antico" is the main clause of fear: temo (present 'I fear') + di perder viver (infinitives 'to lose / to live' here 'to lose my life') + tra coloro ('among those') + relative clause che ... chiameranno (future 'will call'); the sequence expresses Dante's concern about being out of step with future readers.
segments.paradiso-17-g109: "La luce in che rideva il mio tesoro ch’io trovai lì," is a relative description: La luce ('the light') + in che rideva ('in which was laughing/smiling') + il mio tesoro ('my treasure') + ch’io trovai lì (relative 'which I found there'); rideva is imperfect indicating continuous past action.
segments.paradiso-17-g110: "si fé prima corusca," uses si + fe' (fé, passato remoto of fare) to mean 'it made itself/it became' and corusca (feminine adjective/noun 'a flash/sparkle'); prima ('first') orders the sequence — 'it first flashed.'
segments.paradiso-17-g111: "quale a raggio di sole specchio d’oro;" is a simile: quale ('such as') + a raggio di sole ('to/at a ray of sun') + specchio d'oro ('a mirror of gold') — poetic image comparing the flashing light to a golden mirror reflecting a sunbeam.
segments.paradiso-17-g112: "indi rispuose:" indi (archaic 'then' or 'thence') followed by rispuose (passato remoto of rispondere 'it answered') introduces the reply; indi is an adverb of sequence (here the supplied dictionary sense was mismatched, so it's treated as archaic 'then').
segments.paradiso-17-g113: The quoted line is a declarative sentence: Coscïenza fusca ('a darkened conscience') or de la propria o de l’altrui vergogna ('whether of one's own shame or another's') pur sentirà ('will nevertheless feel') la tua parola brusca ('your harsh word'). The verb sentirà is future indicative; fusca and other adjectives are poetic/archaic forms describing the conscience.
segments.paradiso-17-g114: "Ma nondimen," is a concessive connective: Ma ('but') + nondimen (archaic 'nevertheless'), introducing a contrast to the previous caution.
segments.paradiso-17-g115: "rimossa ogne menzogna," is an absolute participial phrase with rimossa (past participle 'removed') modifying the following main clause, and ogne menzogna ('every falsehood') as its object — 'with every falsehood removed.'
segments.paradiso-17-g116: "tutta tua visïon fa manifesta;" is the main clause: tutta ('whole') tua visïon ('your vision') + fa (present 'makes' or 'renders') + manifesta (adjective 'manifest/clear') — 'your entire vision is made manifest.'
segments.paradiso-17-g117: "e lascia pur grattar dov’ è la rogna." is an imperative/advisory: e ('and') + lascia pur ('go ahead/leave it be' — lascia here can be an imperative or permissive 'let/leave') + grattar ('to scratch' in the infinitive, poetic) + dov' è la rogna ('where the scab/mange is'); the phrase is proverbial: 'scratch where the sore is.'

sentences.paradiso-17-s5: Not by circumlocution — the kind in which the foolish people were already getting entangled before the Lamb of God, who takes away their sins, was slain — but in clear words and in precise Latin that fatherly Love answered, closed and appearing in his own smile: "Contingency, which does not extend outside the book of your matter, is all portrayed in the eternal presence; necessity therefore does not take hold from that except as from the face in which a ship is reflected that goes down a torrent."
sentences.paradiso-17-s6: From there — just as sweet harmony from an organ comes to the ear — the time that prepares itself for you comes into my sight.
sentences.paradiso-17-s7: Just as Hippolytus left Athens because of the pitiless and treacherous stepmother, so it is fitting that you leave Florence.
sentences.paradiso-17-s8: This is desired and this is already being sought, and soon it will be accomplished for whoever thinks this, there where Christ plies his trade all day long.
sentences.paradiso-17-s9: Guilt will follow the offended party in outcry, as is wont; but vengeance will be witness to the truth that dispenses it.
sentences.paradiso-17-s10: You will leave every beloved thing more dearly; and this is that arrow which the bow of exile shoots first.
sentences.paradiso-17-s11: You will learn how other people's bread tastes of salt, and how hard a road it is to go down and to go up another's stairs.
sentences.paradiso-17-s12: And what will weigh most upon your shoulders will be the wicked and worthless company with which you will fall into this valley; which, wholly ungrateful, wholly mad and impious, will set itself against you; but soon after she, not you, will have her temple reddened.
sentences.paradiso-17-s25: For if your voice is troublesome at first hearing, it will later leave vital nourishment once it has been digested.
sentences.paradiso-17-s26: This cry of yours will act like the wind, which strikes the highest peaks the more; and that is no small argument of honor.
sentences.paradiso-17-s27: Therefore you have been shown in these spheres, both on the mount and in the doleful valley, even the souls who are famous; for the spirit of him who hears neither rests nor establishes firm faith — for example, if its root is unknown and hidden — nor (does it rest) on any other argument that does not seem so.
sentences.paradiso-17-s17: His magnificences will still be known, so that his enemies will not be able to keep their tongues silent about them.
sentences.paradiso-17-s18: He awaits you, and his benefices; through him many people will be changed, the rich and beggars exchanging their condition; and they will carry it written in his mind, and you will not say it»; and he said incredible things to those who were present.
sentences.paradiso-17-s19: Then he came: "Son, these are the glosses of what was told to you; here are the ambushes that lie hidden a few turns behind."
sentences.paradiso-17-s20: I do not want, however, that envy fall upon your neighbors, since your life will henceforth go further than the punishment of their perfidies.
sentences.paradiso-17-s1: Like one who went to Climenè to make sure of what he had heard concerning himself — what fathers still do for their needy children — such was I, and such I was perceived both by Beatrice and by the holy lamp that had earlier changed its place for me.
sentences.paradiso-17-s2: Therefore my lady said to me, 'Send forth the blaze of your desire,' she told me, 'so that it may come forth well stamped by the inward imprint: not that our knowledge may increase through your speaking, but so that you may dare to speak of your thirst, so that man may mingle you.'
sentences.paradiso-17-s3: “O my dear pious one, who are so unaccustomed to yourself, for as earthly minds see that two obtuse angles cannot fit into a triangle, so you see contingent things before they are in themselves, by gazing at the point to which all times are present; while I was joined to Virgil upon the mountain that cares for souls and, descending into the nether world, words concerning my future life were said to me — grave words — although I feel myself well braced against the blows of fortune; therefore my will would be satisfied to understand what fate approaches me: for a foreseen bolt comes more slowly.”
sentences.paradiso-17-s4: Thus I answered to that very light which had earlier spoken to me; and, as Beatrice wished, my desire was made known.
sentences.paradiso-17-s13: His trial will furnish the proof of his beastliness; so that it will be pleasing to you to have earned your share by your own doing.
sentences.paradiso-17-s14: Your first refuge and first lodging will be the courtesy of the great Lombard who carries the holy bird on the ladder;
sentences.paradiso-17-s15: With him you will see the man who was impressed at his birth so strongly by this star that his works became remarkable.
sentences.paradiso-17-s16: People are not yet aware of it because of his young age, for these spheres have been turning around him for only nine years; but before the Gascon deceives the lofty Henry, sparks of his virtue will be shown in his not caring for silver nor for anxieties.
sentences.paradiso-17-s21: Afterwards, remaining silent, the holy soul showed itself ready to put the pattern into that cloth which I had set up for it; I began, like one who longs, doubting, to ask counsel from a person who sees and wishes straightforwardly and loves: "I plainly see, my father, how time spurs itself toward me to deal me such a blow, which is more grievous to whoever abandons himself more; therefore, by providence it is good that I arm myself, so that, if a place dearer to me is taken away, I should not lose the others through my verses."
sentences.paradiso-17-s22: Down through the world without an end of bitterness, and over the mountain whose fair summit my lady’s eyes raised me to, and afterwards through the heavens, from light to light, I have learned what — if I recount it — will taste to many like a strong sourness; and if in truth I am a timid friend, I fear to lose my life among those who will call this time ancient.
sentences.paradiso-17-s23: The light in which my treasure was smiling — which I found there — first flashed like a golden mirror to a ray of sun; then it answered: "A darkened conscience, whether from one’s own shame or from another’s, will nevertheless feel your harsh word."
sentences.paradiso-17-s24: But nonetheless, with every falsehood removed, your whole vision becomes manifest; and go ahead and scratch where the scab is.
